11 February 2026

No

If there are positions or rights to subscribe to disclose in more than one class of relevant securities of the offeror or offeree named in 1(c), copy table 2(a) or (b) (as appropriate) for each additional class of relevant security.

4.         OTHER INFORMATION

(a)        Indemnity and other dealing arrangements

  • Details of any indemnity or option arrangement, or any agreement or understanding, formal or informal, relating to relevant securities which may be an inducement to deal or refrain from dealing entered into by the person making the disclosure and any party to the offer or any person acting in concert with a party to the offer:

    Irrevocable commitments and letters of intent should not be included.  If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • None

(b)        Agreements, arrangements or understandings relating to options or derivatives

  • Details of any agreement, arrangement or understanding, formal or informal, between the person making the disclosure and any other person relating to:

    (i)  the voting rights of any relevant securities under any option; or

    (ii) the voting rights or future acquisition or disposal of any relevant securities to which any derivative is referenced:

    If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • No

12 February 2026

data.title: Nuveen, Schroders CEOs Play Down AI Fears After Deal to Form Asset-Management Powerhouse — Interview
data.description:
(14:55 GMT) *Schroders to Deliver on CMD Targets Amid Nuveen Acquisition, CEO Says
(14:55 GMT) *Schroders Deal Puts Nuveen in Industry's Driving Seat, Schroders CEO Says
(14:55 GMT) *AI Is Opportunity to Innovate, Grow, Be Efficient in Asset Management, Nuveen CEO Says
(14:55 GMT) *AI Is Important to Nuveen, Schroders Businesses, Nuveen CEO Says
(14:55 GMT) Nuveen, Schroders CEOs Play Down AI Fears After Deal to Form Asset-Management Powerhouse — Interview

By Elena Vardon

The chief executives of asset managers Nuveen and Schroders sought to soothe investors' concerns about the threat of artificial intelligence to the sector, after agreeing to join forces in a $13.5 billion deal that they said would put the enlarged group in the industry's driver's seat.

Chicago-headquartered Nuveen is set to buy Britain's Schroders in a cash deal announced on Thursday that will see a revered name in the City of London, with roots dating back more than two centuries, come under control of the asset-management arm of Teachers Insurance and Annuity Association of America.

The deal coincides with a selloff in wealth and asset managers' shares on both sides of the Atlantic earlier this week on fears that AI could commoditize financial advice and upend their business model. Investors have grown increasingly concerned as AI startups roll out automated planning tools that showcase real-world applications of the technology and risk undercutting traditional fees, mirroring recent jitters in the data, software and insurance industries.

"I think we're obsessing about AI when, really, we're reimagining the industry," Schroders's chief executive officer, Richard Oldfield, said in an interview. There is too much talk about the hammer when people should be focusing on the blueprint for the house, he said.

Oldfield, who will stay on as Schroders's CEO after the Nuveen acquisition, downplayed the threat of AI on its business model.

"I'm not sure you're going to convince someone who's got millions of dollars' worth of savings to deal with an AI chatbot," he said, describing the idea as somewhat extreme.

Nuveen's CEO, William Huffman, highlighted the importance of AI as an efficiency driver and said the technology could also help asset managers to innovate and grow.

"I don't know if [the market reaction is] overblown or not…People get excited about different topics," he said in the same interview. "AI will help us continue to grow our organization and serve our clients even better."

The Nuveen-Schroders deal reflects the value of traditional asset management, following other transactions that were more defensive in nature, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ben Bathurst wrote in a note to clients.

The combined group will oversee nearly $2.5 trillion in client assets across wealth and institutional channels and have a foothold in more than 40 markets, the companies said.

The deal is backed by the U.K. company's largest shareholder--the founding Schroder family--and marks the exit of one of the most established names of London's bourse, having been independent since its creation in 1804.

"I don't recognize any sense that this is about taking a step backwards. I think it's a massive step forward in terms of Nuveen in the driving seat of the industry as we go forward," Oldfield said.

Nuveen is set to keep the Schroders brand and make London the base for its non-U.S. headquarters.

Both Oldfield and Huffman hailed the compatibility of the businesses and their limited overlap.

"We're going to take Nuveen's strength in private markets and alternatives, in U.S. distribution, in fixed income, and we're going to put that together with Schroders's capabilities and heritage in public markets," Oldfield said. "They're literally two jigsaw pieces that fit together beautifully."

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: Amazon.com Currently Down Eight Consecutive Days, on Track for Longest Losing Streak Since August 2019 — Data Talk
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) is currently at $202.52, down $1.56 or 0.76%

  • Would be lowest close since May 23, 2025, when it closed at $200.99
  • On pace for largest percent decrease since Feb. 11, 2026, when it fell 1.39%
  • Currently down 11 of the past 12 days
  • Currently down eight consecutive days; down 16.64% over this period
  • Longest losing streak since Aug. 5, 2019, when it fell for eight straight trading days
  • Worst eight day stretch since the eight days ending April 4, 2025, when it fell 16.87%
  • Down 15.37% month-to-date
  • Down 12.26% year-to-date
  • Down 20.27% from its all-time closing high of $254.00 on Nov. 3, 2025
  • Down 12.09% from 52 weeks ago (Feb. 13, 2025), when it closed at $230.37
  • Down 20.27% from its 52-week closing high of $254.00 on Nov. 3, 2025
  • Up 21.04% from its 52-week closing low of $167.32 on April 21, 2025
  • Traded as low as $202.40
  • Down 0.82% at today's intraday low
  • Sixth most active stock in the Nasdaq 100 today
  • Subtracted 9.6 points from the DJIA so far today

All data as of 9:46:19 AM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: TSX Edges Lower
data.description:
The S&P TSX Composite edged down 0.1% to below the 33,250 on Thursday, falling further from record highs as weakness in technology and select commodity names offset strength in financials and utilities.

Shopify fell around 4% despite recent rating upgrades, while Constellation Software dropped over 2.5% and Celestica lost around 3.5%.

Gold miners were broadly softer, with Barrick down 1.5%, Agnico Eagle off 1% and Kinross sliding 1.8%, even as bullion prices remain elevated this year.

In contrast, banks provided support, with Royal Bank rising 0.8%, TD up 0.7%, Scotiabank gaining 0.5% and National Bank adding 0.4%, reflecting resilience in the financial sector.

Sun Life surged nearly 5% after earnings, while Manulife fell 2.7% on weaker quarterly profit, sending mixed signals from insurers.

Energy shares were mixed as Suncor and Cenovus posted modest gains while Canadian Natural slipped.

data.title: Tesla Stock Rises. It's Going for a 5-Day Winning Streak. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Tesla stock rose early Thursday, continuing its recent strong performance.

Shares of the electric-vehicle maker were up 0.2% at $429.26 in premarket trading, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were both up about 0.3%.

If Tesla stock closes in the green, it will mark five consecutive days of gains. Tesla stock has added about 8% over its four-day run but is still about $3 below its Jan. 28 close, before Tesla reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter earnings.

Since then, the news has been dominated by one of CEO Elon Musk's other companies, SpaceX. It acquired Musk's AI company xAI on the way to a likely midyear initial public offering.

Musk talked about reorganizing xAI to "improve speed of execution" on Wednesday, adding, "As a company grows, especially as quickly as xAI, the structure must evolve just like any living organism."

He's done that before. Musk cut about 10% of Tesla's workforce in 2024. "We've had a long period of prosperity from 2019 to now, and so if the company...organizationally is 5% wrong per year, that accumulates to 25%, 30% of inefficiency," said Musk at the time. "We've made some corrections along the way. But it is time to reorganize the company for the next phase of growth."

As for Tesla, investors are waiting for something to move the stock. The likeliest candidates are robo-taxis and robots. Tesla plans to expand its robo-taxi service to nine cities in the first half of 2026. It also plans to make the third generation of its humanoid robot, Optimus, this year.

Expansion and the robot haven't arrived yet.

Coming into Thursday trading, Tesla stock was up 30% over the past 12 months.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: FedEx Has Big Goals That Deserve a Bigger Valuation, If Investors Believe Them. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

FedEx meets with investors on Thursday to review its long-term plans for shareholder value.

Investors should be encouraged.

For starters, FedEx said fiscal third-quarter earnings should "exceed the consensus average." That means earnings per share of about $4 or higher.

FedEx stock was down 0.4% at $365.88 in premarket trading, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were up about 0.2%.

The earnings beat was the appetizer. In fiscal year 2029, FedEx expects to produce revenue of $98 billion, operating income of $8 billion, and free cash flow of $6 billion. All those metrics are ahead of Wall Street estimates.

Backing out FedEx Freight, which is due to be spun off in June and will host its own investors day in April, analysts project sales of about $93 billion, operating income of about $6.4 billion, and free cash flow of less than $6 billion, according to FactSet.

The long-term goal implies annual sales growth of about 4%, about 1 percentage point better than current estimates.

Getting there will depend on growing higher-profit businesses, such as healthcare and aerospace, as well as squeezing out more costs through network simplification and automation.

"FedEx is now entering a new era as we build the most flexible, efficient, and intelligent network in history. Our vision is simple: to make supply chains smarter for everyone," CEO Raj Subramaniam said in a news release. The company's digital intelligence "is a true force multiplier that will support durable value with profitable growth, higher margins, stronger cash generation, and increased returns for our stockholders."

Things look good. How the stock will trade is anyone's guess. Expectations are running high. Coming into Thursday trading, FedEx stock was up an impressive 27% year to date, leaving shares trading for about 18 times earnings expected over the coming 12 months.

That's an elevated PE ratio for FedEx, but below the 22 times multiple for the S&P 500. FedEx's goals imply annual operating profit growth well north of 10%. That, if investors believe it, would justify, at least, a market multiple.

Investors typically take a while to be convinced. But the possibility of improved profitability and cash flow should keep them interested in FedEx for the foreseeable future.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Amazon Currently Down Eight Consecutive Days, On Pace for Longest Losing Streak Since August 2019 — Data Talk
data.description:

  • Would be lowest close since May 23, 2025, when it closed at $200.99
  • On pace for largest percent decrease since Feb. 11, 2026, when it fell 1.39%

  • Currently down eight consecutive days; down 16.64% over this period

  • Down 15.37% month-to-date
  • Down 12.26% year-to-date

data.title: PD: Q4 revenue up 2% but net loss driven by one-time charges; 2026 targets capex and buybacks
data.description:
Q4 2025 saw revenue rise 2% to $479M and Adjusted EBITDA up 5% to $126M, but a $42M net loss was recorded due to non-cash charges. Annual revenue fell 3% to $1.84B, with $101M in debt reduction and $76M in share buybacks. 2026 guidance targets $245M in capex and up to 50% of free cash flow for repurchases.
